html:
Aisha: a goat ate the stoning verse and the ten-sucklings verse Abrogation Scripture Integrity Strong Ibn Majah #1944
"The verse of stoning and of suckling an adult ten times was revealed, and they were (written) on a paper and kept under my pillow. When the Messenger of Allah died and we were preoccupied with his death, a tame goat came in and ate away the paper."

What the hadith says

Aisha reports that two Quranic verses — the stoning verse and the ten-sucklings verse — were written on paper, stored under her pillow, and eaten by a domestic goat while the household was distracted by the Prophet's death. Both verses were law; neither survived into the written Quran.

Why this is a problem

  1. Q 15:9's preservation guarantee is defeated by a farmyard animal. "We have sent down the Reminder and We will protect it" is directly falsified — two revealed verses were physically eaten before they could be compiled. The goat succeeded where human enemies failed.
  2. Sunni penal law imposes stoning for adultery on the basis of a verse eaten by a goat. Classical jurisprudence relies on hadith testimony that the verse once existed. A capital punishment rule runs on the testimony that its scriptural basis was destroyed by livestock.

Why it fails

The "pre-planned abrogation" framing turns a domesticated livestock event into a divinely orchestrated publication mechanism. More critically, the naskh al-tilawa doctrine means Islam imposes the death penalty for adultery on the basis of a verse that no longer exists, preserved only by hadith attestation that it once did. The Quran claims divine preservation; the tradition concedes two legal verses were not preserved; the apologetic reframes that failure as a theological feature.

Houris in a hollowed-pearl tent 60 miles wide — the male believer circulates among them Paradise Sexual Issues Strong Ibn Majah #4336
"In Paradise the believer will have a tent made from a single hollowed pearl, its width sixty miles. In it will be his family; he will circulate among them."

What the hadith says

Each male believer in paradise receives a private tent carved from a single pearl, 60 miles across, populated with wives and houris among whom he circulates. Bukhari (#4879) carries the same tradition; Ibn Majah adds further attestation.

Why this is a problem

  1. Paradise is designed around male sexual access at cosmic scale. The primary specific reward for male believers is a 60-mile tent full of women to circulate among. No equivalent female-centred paradise promise exists in the canonical corpus.
  2. The Sahihayn-tier parallel in Bukhari forecloses dismissal. Classical commentators (Ibn Hajar, al-Nawawi) read it literally. When a religious tradition specifies its highest reward, that specification reveals what the tradition most values — and the canonical answer here is unlimited, scaled-up, supernatural sexual access for men.

Why it fails

Classical commentators read the 60-mile pearl tent literally — al-Nawawi and Ibn Hajar do not treat "sixty miles" as a figure of speech. The "7th-century imagery" defense concedes the description is culturally constructed; a timelessly authoritative revelation cannot simultaneously be calibrated for one cultural moment's imagination. The absence of any parallel female-centred promise remains a structural asymmetry regardless of how the houri imagery is interpreted.

Ibn Khatal killed clinging to the Ka'ba cloth — by prophetic command Warfare & Jihad Prophetic Character Strong Ibn Majah #2805
"It was said to the Prophet: 'Ibn Khatal is clinging to the covering of the Ka'ba.' He said: 'Kill him.'"

What the hadith says

During the Conquest of Mecca, Ibn Khatal sought sanctuary by clinging to the Ka'ba covering — traditionally inviolable. Muhammad issued the execution order without hesitation. Bukhari (#1846) and Abu Dawud both preserve the account; classical sources also identify among those ordered killed a slave girl who had sung satirical songs about Muhammad.

Why this is a problem

  1. The Ka'ba's traditional sanctuary status was explicitly overridden. Muhammad's personal authority superseded even the sacred precinct's protection — establishing that a religious leader's execution order can override established sanctuary rights.
  2. The hit list includes a satirist. Classical sources identify among those ordered killed a woman who had composed satirical songs about the Prophet — a precedent for executing critics cited across Islamic history and in contemporary blasphemy jurisprudence.

Why it fails

The hadith is preserved as a Prophetic command without restriction to its exceptional character — it is transmitted as a ruled case, not a one-time anomaly. The slave girl's case — execution for satirical songs — has been cited explicitly in Islamic history to justify killing critics and blasphemers. A prophet whose mercy-advertised city-conquest included a personalised execution list for apostates, murderers, and satirists used mercy as the narrative and violence as the method.

"Kill the doer and the one it is done to" — homosexual act LGBTQ / Gender Hudud Strong Ibn Majah #2561
"Whoever you find doing the act of the people of Lot — kill the doer and the one it is being done to."

What the hadith says

Ibn Majah preserves the death-for-homosexuality command explicitly naming both the active and passive partner regardless of consent.

Why this is a problem

  1. Mutual punishment for consensual adult intimacy. Both parties — regardless of consent — face death. The passive partner includes anyone coerced. This hadith, cross-attested in Tirmidhi and Abu Dawud, is cited in active capital sentences in Iran, Saudi Arabia, and Yemen.
  2. No Quranic verse prescribes this penalty. The death rule is entirely hadith-derived. Classical fiqh built capital punishment for homosexuality on this chain alone, without Quranic support for the specific sentence.

Why it fails

Chain disputes do not remove the hadith from active jurisprudential use — Ibn Majah, Tirmidhi, and Abu Dawud all preserve parallel versions, giving the rule multi-collection weight that classical Shafi'i, Hanbali, and Maliki schools have relied upon. The "Quranic flogging-precedence" argument requires treating this hadith as abrogated, which the majority classical position does not accept.

Selling a pregnant concubine — permitted, then forbidden Slavery & Captives Sexual Issues Strong Ibn Majah #2517
"The Prophet forbade selling pregnant she-slaves, but Umar and Ali debated exceptions."

What the hadith says

Ibn Majah records the early Companions debating whether pregnant enslaved women could be sold. The debate is juristic, not abolitionist — the question is when sale is permissible, not whether ownership itself is.

Why this is a problem

  1. Slave trading is preserved as a canonical religious discussion. The umm walad (mother of owner's child) doctrine protects one specific category of slave from resale — but leaves slavery, concubinage, and the sexual-access structure entirely intact.
  2. A legal tradition that debates the resale of pregnant slaves has not outgrown the institution — it has refined it. Every classical school codified concubinage and slave-trading in detail across fourteen centuries.

Why it fails

The umm walad doctrine operates entirely within the institution it protects. It left slavery, concubinage, and the sexual-access structure intact while refining one narrow resale rule. The "progressive tightening" framing requires a gradualism fourteen centuries of jurisprudence did not deliver. A legal tradition whose canonical commentary debates the resale of pregnant slaves has normalised the institution, not outgrown it.

Night raids — "they are from them" when women and children die Warfare & Jihad Moral Problems Strong Ibn Majah #2840
"It was said: What of the women and children of the idolaters who are killed during the night raid? He said: 'They are from them.'"

What the hadith says

Women and children killed during night raids on polytheist settlements are morally classified with the combatants — their deaths are permitted collateral. Belonging to the enemy community is sufficient; no individual threat assessment is required.

Why this is a problem

  1. Non-combatant status is erased by kinship. The principle makes collective membership the operative criterion for permissible killing — a doctrine with obvious catastrophic potential in actual warfare.
  2. The same ruling appears in Muslim and Bukhari, giving it the cross-collection weight of settled legal doctrine. Classical jihad-jurisprudence derived a doctrine of permissible incidental killing of non-combatants from this and parallel hadiths, which remains available in contemporary jihadist legal literature.

Why it fails

The "narrow exception" reading requires the broader prohibition to override this hadith rather than vice versa — a juristic choice, not a textual necessity. The plain answer — "they are from them" — does not qualify timing, context, or inevitability. It makes collective membership the operative criterion, and classical jihad-jurisprudence used it that way.

Aisha played with dolls — winged horses — in Muhammad's presence Child Marriage Prophetic Character Strong Ibn Majah #1975
"I used to play with dolls in the presence of the Prophet. I had a horse that had two wings made of cloth. He smiled and said: 'What is this?' I said: 'A horse.' He said: 'And what is on it?' I said: 'Wings.' He said: 'A horse with wings?' I said: 'Have you not heard that Sulayman had horses with wings?' So he laughed."

What the hadith says

Aisha narrates playing with toy horses in Muhammad's household — she was clearly a child during their cohabitation. She was not recalling a pre-marital girlhood; she was describing her married life in the Prophet's household.

Why this is a problem

  1. First-person preservation makes the apologetic rescue impossible. This is Aisha's own voice, documenting a childhood developmental stage occurring within the marriage. A household where a wife is the age of her dolls has a moral profile the canonical record has documented in detail.
  2. Picture-making was elsewhere forbidden, but exempted for child-Aisha. The canonical tradition preserves both the prohibition and the exception without moral comment on what the exception reveals about the wife's age.

Why it fails

The preservation is the problem. Apologetic moves must either accept the consummation age and explain away the doll-playing, or accept the doll-playing and revise the consummation age — the tradition preserves both, and the willingness to preserve both without editorial discomfort reveals the community's ethical assumptions about the marriage.

A father can marry off his pre-pubescent daughter without her consent Child Marriage Women Governance Strong Ibn Majah #1874
"The father is more entitled than the virgin in deciding her marriage; her silence is her consent."

What the hadith says

A virgin daughter's father can bind her in marriage; silence is legally consent. For a girl too young to understand, this operationalises parental authority as a complete substitute for the daughter's will.

Why this is a problem

  1. Silence as consent is structurally coercive. A terrified, intimidated, or uncomprehending girl has no structural means to object — her silence is captured as agreement regardless of its actual content.
  2. The doctrine has been applied to child marriage for 1,400 years. Contemporary jurisdictions permitting child marriage in Yemen, Afghanistan, and parts of Nigeria cite the classical wilaya al-ijbar doctrine directly derived from this and parallel hadiths.

Why it fails

The wilaya al-ijbar doctrine is the classical law, not a modern misreading. Its operational logic — father's marriage-decision authority; silence interpreted as consent — directly underwrote child marriage across Islamic history. Modern narrowing is a welcome reform against the classical framework, not a retrieval of it. A legal system that takes silence as agreement has defined consent as the absence of rebellion, which is the definition of coercion.

A Jew cast a spell on Muhammad — a hair and comb in a well Prophetic Character Magic & Occult Strong Ibn Majah #3545
"A Jew cast a spell on the Prophet, and he fell ill from it."

What the hadith says

Ibn Majah preserves the bewitchment of Muhammad by a Jewish sorcerer — a magical illness that incapacitated the Prophet for an extended period. A hair and comb were buried in a well; Muhammad experienced false memories for months before surahs al-Falaq and al-Nas were revealed as a cure.

Why this is a problem

  1. Magic working on a prophet undermines the Quran's protection promise. Q 5:67 states Allah will protect Muhammad from the people. A successful months-long bewitchment directly contradicts that claim.
  2. Any revelations during the bewitched period carry potential taint. If his memory could be falsely altered for months, certainty about revelations received during that window is epistemically compromised — a problem the tradition cannot fully resolve.

Why it fails

The apologetic requires a clean line between "mundane perception" and "prophetic reception" that the hadith does not draw. If a sorcerer could plant false memories for months without divine protection preventing it, the claim that no revelation was tainted is a stipulation, not a verification. Q 5:67's protection promise covers people generally — yet a Jewish sorcerer's interference operated unchecked for a multi-month period.

Six privileges given only to Muhammad — including "victory through terror" Prophetic Privileges Warfare & Jihad Strong Ibn Majah #567
"I have been given six things no prophet before me was given: comprehensive speech; victory through terror; war booty made lawful; the earth made pure and a place of worship; sent to all mankind; and the seal of prophethood."

What the hadith says

Muhammad lists six divine gifts unique to his prophethood, including victory through terror (al-ru'b) and the lawfulness of war booty — both explicitly unavailable to prior prophets.

Why this is a problem

  1. "Victory by terror" is listed as a divine gift, not a regrettable side-effect. Al-ru'b is presented alongside the seal of prophethood as a positive distinguishing mark of Muhammad's mission. The Bukhari parallel (#2977) frames it as "I have been helped by terror against my enemy from a distance of a month's journey."
  2. "Booty made lawful" frames war spoils as a special divine dispensation. The explicit contrast with all previous prophets treats plunder as a Muhammadan exception — a self-description that defines prophethood at the intersection of intimidation and acquisition.

Why it fails

The semantic range of ru'b in these hadiths is military psychological intimidation, not mere legitimate authority. Listing it alongside universal prophethood and the seal of divine mission frames terror-induction as a prophetic credential. A self-description that includes war-booty entitlement and enemy-terror as divine gifts has defined its prophethood at the intersection of intimidation and acquisition.

Aisha taken from a swing to consummate marriage Child Marriage Prophetic Character Strong Ibn Majah #1876
"Umm Ruman came to me — I was on a swing with my girlfriends. She called me, washed my face with water, and took me into the house."

What the hadith says

Aisha's own memory of being collected from play, washed, and delivered for consummation — preserved across multiple collections as the age-9 account. She describes it as a child would remember an interruption of play.

Why this is a problem

  1. The scene is childhood interrupted. The narrator was a child playing on a swing with girlfriends. No adult recognition of what was happening is present in her narration.
  2. First-person preservation makes the apologetic rescue impossible. This is Aisha's own voice — making the developmental disjunction between bride and child undeniable. The tradition preserved it without apparent editorial discomfort, which reveals the community's ethical assumptions.

Why it fails

The cultural-normalcy framing concedes that the ethics are historical rather than eternal. A "marriage" whose most vivid memory is the loss of a swing has preserved, in the bride's own voice, the developmental disjunction that apologetics cannot paper over. Aisha's narration without adult recognition is not evidence of comfort — it is evidence of a child's perspective, which is precisely the problem.

Angels curse a wife who refuses bed until morning Women Moral Problems Strong Ibn Majah #1853
"If a husband calls his wife to his bed and she refuses, and he spends the night angry with her, the angels curse her until morning."

What the hadith says

A wife who refuses sex faces continuous angelic cursing for the duration of the night. The trigger is the husband's anger — not an objective offense.

Why this is a problem

  1. Consent is structurally eliminated from the marital bed. The husband's subjective anger — not any harm caused — activates a divine sanction against refusal. This cross-collection hadith (Bukhari, Muslim, and Ibn Majah) is settled canonical doctrine.
  2. The doctrine of tamkeen flows from this. Classical jurisprudence derived the doctrine that sexual access is the husband's enforceable right from this and parallel hadiths, effectively removing the wife's consent from the legal framework of marriage.

Why it fails

The "legitimate reasons" exception is juristically elaborated but absent from the hadith's plain text. The curse falls on the wife whose refusal angers the husband — full stop. A heavens whose angels curse a wife for saying no has theologically sanctified marital coercion, whatever later juristic exceptions soften the rule's edges.
